Empires & Puzzles Otis Review: The Abyss Hunter's Slow Burn

Otis charges into the arena with a massive axe and an even bigger attitude. But does this slow-speed Abyss Hunter have what it takes to keep up with the lightning-fast meta, or is he just a lot of bull? We break down his bleeding, healing reduction, and whether he's still a contender.
Otis Review: A Bull in a China Shop... Moving in Slow Motion
Otis: The Slow-Burning Bull of the Abyss

Otis is a 5-star Fire hero from the Abyss Hunter family, originally released during the Covenant Summon in late 2022. As a Barbarian class hero, he’s built to inflict bleeding wounds on his enemies, literally and figuratively. With a slow mana speed, he fits the classic archetype of a "nuke" hero—hard to charge, but devastating when he finally goes off. Or at least, that was the idea back when 888 Power was impressive. Today, he’s a bit like a vintage muscle car: loud, heavy, and potentially powerful, but lacking the modern safety features (stats) of the newer models.

Otis is an absolute unit. He’s a Minotaur-esque warrior clad in heavy crimson armor, wielding a flaming sword that looks like it weighs more than most 3-star heroes. The glowing golden accents and the sheer bulk of the character design scream "tank," even if his stats don't quite back that up anymore. He looks like the kind of guy who doesn't knock on doors; he just walks through the wall.
When Otis was released, his stats were top-tier. However, the game has evolved rapidly.
His base attack of 861 (1061 at LB2) is now overshadowed by modern attackers who regularly push past 1300 base attack.
His survivability is the biggest concern. With LB2 health sitting around 1925, he is significantly squishier than modern heroes who boast 2200+ health
In the current meta, Otis's stats are considered
. He requires heavy investment (Emblems, Limit Breaks) just to survive long enough to fire his slow skill.
Otis’s special skill is a "kitchen sink" of offensive pressure, but it comes with the heavy price tag of Slow mana.

370% damage to all enemies is a solid multiplier. It’s comparable to Khufu or other heavy hitters. However, because his base attack stat has fallen behind, the actual damage output isn't as terrifying as it used to be.
The scaling Bleed damage (up to ~575 over 4 turns) is a nice bonus, especially since it refreshes and grows. It pairs perfectly with his Barbarian class bleed, potentially stacking multiple DoTs.

This is arguably the most dangerous part of his kit. A -70% healing reduction that scales to -100% is a death sentence for teams relying on healers to recover. If Otis fires, the enemy team is essentially locked out of recovery for 4 turns.
Slow (12 tiles). With a Lv. 23 Mana Troop, he needs 11 tiles. To get him to 9 tiles (functional Average) requires a huge amount of mana support (Magic Troops, Bards, etc.).
370% x 5 enemies = 1850% total damage output. This is high efficiency per tile

The scaling from -70% to -100% healing means that by turn 3 and 4 of the ailment, enemies are receiving literally zero healing. This counters superior revive talents and heavy healers like Hathor or newer powerhouses.
Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Abyss Hunter family and passives add some much-needed utility to his slow kit.
The defense bonus is decent if you run him with other Abyss Hunters, but given the rarity of these heroes in modern defenses, you likely won't see this active often.
This is excellent. Transferring his newest status ailment to an enemy upon casting is a great "reverse cleanse." If he’s clean, he heals for 12%. It helps his survivability slightly.
Applies a Hunter's Mark for extra DoT. It’s not game-changing damage, but every bit adds up.
gives him a boost to attack at the start of the battle. For a slow hero, this is a bit awkward because the Rage might expire or be less relevant by the time he actually charges.
Upon release, Otis was seen as a "balanced" alternative to the terrifying Khufu. He was a staple in Rush Wars and a solid option for defense teams that lacked the premier heavy hitters.
The stat gap is undeniable. Otis essentially has 4-star stats compared to the gods of 2026. His slow speed makes him a liability in standard raid formats where speed is king.
Otis has been relegated to a

. In Very Fast modes, his skill is devastating because the tile penalty is removed. Outside of Rush, he is too slow and too fragile to be reliable in top-tier play. He is a "Bench" hero for most situations but a "Star" for that one specific rule set.

his spell (which wins games via healing prevention), keeping him alive is priority number one. However, his class is Barbarian, which benefits from high attack for bleed damage.
Maximizes his chances of firing his slow skill. Given his outdated stats, he needs every point of defense he can get.

He might die before he ever gets to swing that axe.
path is recommended. A dead Otis deals zero damage. His utility comes from the healing reduction and the hit-all; ensuring he survives to use it is key.
In Very Fast wars, he is a top-tier threat.
The scaling healing reduction shuts down sustain teams completely.
The ability to transfer an ailment or heal himself gives him a slight edge in durability.

Without Rush rules, 12 tiles is an eternity in the current meta.
He is significantly squishier than modern heroes, making him an easy target for fast snipers.
His Bleed and Healing Debuff are cleansable (unless he's paired with an ailment blocker).
The "start low, increase later" mechanic on his bleed is less effective than immediate damage, as it gives enemies time to find a cleanse.
This is his home. He shines when he charges in 6.5 tiles.

Ludwig gives Otis the mana he desperately needs. If Ludwig fires, Otis fires, and the enemy team melts.
Emilio blocks ailments and burns, Otis bleeds and debuffs healing, Russell gains mana from the burns and never misses.
Rigard, Ariel, or any modern cleanser wipes Otis's effects instantly.
Hel, Proteus, or anyone who stops him from gaining mana makes him a statue.

Running too many slow heroes together is a recipe for disaster unless it's Rush.

If you pulled Otis early in your E&P journey, he is a fantastic "boss killer" for map stages. His high damage and healing reduction will help you clear tough levels. Level him up, but be wary of over-investing in limit breaks until you have a deeper roster.
Otis is a niche tool for your Rush War roster. If you have Khufu, Quintin, or modern hit-all nukes, Otis might feel redundant. He is
worth Alpha Aethers unless you are specifically building a specialized Rush defense and lack better options.
The community has always had a love-hate relationship with Otis.
"Compared to him, Porthos looks like a beast," one user joked during his release, highlighting how underwhelming he felt compared to his contemporaries.
Others defended him: "He’s a weaker version of Malin... but dealing 370% to all with bleed and healing reduction is still nothing to sneeze at."
The general consensus? "A dud... unless it's Rush." He lives in the shadow of Khufu and other premier reds.

Otis is a hero out of time. In the fast-paced, high-stat world of modern Empires & Puzzles, he moves like a glacier—massive and dangerous, but easily maneuvered around. While his healing reduction is unique and powerful, his reliance on Slow mana and his aging stat line make him a risky pick for anything other than Rush tournaments. He's not the hunter; he's the trophy on the wall of a bygone era.
Keep him for Rush Wars, but don't expect him to lead your daily raid team.
A detailed review and strategy guide for Otis, the 5-star Fire Barbarian from the Abyss Hunter family in Empires & Puzzles. Is this slow-speed bull still worth the mats in the modern meta?
